header.title: InfraKit
header.subtitle: Production‑grade AWS infra CLI with schema validation & state

summary.plain_english: A command‑line tool that lets developers describe AWS infrastructure in a simple YAML file and deploy it with one command, making complex CloudFormation/CDK setups feel like Terraform.

motivation.quote: I built InfraKit to prove I could design and ship a real infrastructure product with schema validation, idempotent state and CI/CD—not just throw together some CloudFormation scripts.

diagram.alt: Architecture diagram showing CLI → AWS Services APIs → singleton AWS session → S3/DynamoDB state backend

architecture.flow_summary: YAML → Pydantic validation → dependency DAG → plan/apply engine → AWS via singleton session

items: Deploy any stack from a terse YAML file — custom CLI (Typer/Rich) — zero boilerplate for end users
Validate configuration before touching AWS — Pydantic v2 schema — catches errors early
Maintain distributed state with locking — S3 + DynamoDB backend — safe concurrent runs
Show dry‑run diffs and enforce rollback on failure — plan/apply engine — prevents partial deployments
Ship as pip package, Docker image and optional Homebrew formula — modern packaging — usable in under 60 s
Validating a rich YAML schema without forcing users to write verbose JSON required a custom Pydantic model and custom `!ref` syntax parser.
Building a dependency graph capable of detecting circular references took multiple iterations and thorough unit tests.
Designing the state backend to support both local files and S3+DynamoDB locking meant abstracting the storage layer early.
Ensuring every AWS call could be mocked with moto was tricky—some providers needed wrapper stubs and careful error handling.
What I'd do differently: add a higher‑level plugin system sooner so new resource types can be added without touching core logic.
